title: Special tokens
description: Reserved tokenizer symbols for boundaries, padding, masking, and control roles that are not ordinary subwords.
openingSummary: Special tokens are fixed IDs in the vocabulary—such as begin-, end-, pad, and mask slots—that frameworks insert around ordinary text for training and batching.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Special tokens occupy dedicated IDs in the vocabulary for structural roles: sequence start and end, padding to equal batch length, mask tokens for denoising objectives, and tool-specific markers in modern chat models. Tokenizer configs declare their strings and IDs so trainers and inference servers insert them consistently.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Wrong special-token handling shifts every position embedding and attention mask. Adding a new chat marker without resizing embeddings breaks fine-tunes. Debugging generation often starts by verifying that BOS, EOS, and pad IDs match between dataset code and the served tokenizer.json.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: A batch might wrap user text as [BOS] + tokens + [EOS], then pad shorter rows with  IDs so tensor shapes align before the forward pass.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Special tokens are not the same as chat-template role strings—templates decide when to insert them. They are also unrelated to logits bias tricks; special tokens are part of the vocabulary, not post-hoc filters.
